text: The surgeon inserts a Veress needle for insufflation, followed by a trocar placed supraumbilically. The patient is positioned with the head down for diagnostic laparoscopy. The small bowel is pushed upwards, revealing the sigmoid colon and rectum. The uterus is lifted, showing no adnexal pathology or endometriosis. The uterus is then pushed towards the opposite shoulder, and dissection begins approximately four centimeters lateral to the uterus on the round ligament using a 37mm ligature. The round ligament is dissected, followed by the fallopian tube, major salpings, and ovarian ligament, which is coagulated two centimeters lateral to the uterus.

The surgeon dissects the left side of the broad ligament, starting with the round ligament, followed by the fallopian tube and ovarian ligament. Both sides of the adnexal structures are completed. The uterus is then pushed towards the five o'clock position, and the anterior leaf of the broad ligament is separated.

The surgeon uses the left hand to pull the anterior leaf of the peritoneum anteriorly while the right hand pushes the ureter laterally. The uterus is positioned at six o'clock, then moved to seven o'clock as the dissection progresses to the right side. The right side of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament is separated, and the next step involves separating the bladder.

The surgeon identifies the criss-cross pattern of vessels in the vagina and separates the bladder pillar. The posterior peritoneum is then addressed, focusing on the right side of the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. Dissection continues up to the cervical part of the uterosacral ligament, with the peritoneum being pushed laterally. The uterine artery becomes visible, positioned anteriorly to the vein, with the ureter sandwiched between them, showing minimal skeletonization.

The surgeon separates the peritoneum over the arc and cervical part of the uterosacral ligament, followed by irrigation and suction to clean the area. The ascending uterine artery is coagulated, and saline is applied to prevent overheating. The uterine artery is cut on the right side, with the lumen clearly visible. The open jaw of the ligature is used to coagulate the margins to stop any oozing.

The surgeon uses the open jaw of the bipolar instrument to ensure hemostasis on the right side, which is already dissected. The procedure then shifts to the left side, where the surgeon begins the colpotomy using the tip of the harmonic scalpel.

The assistant pushes the colpotomizer into position, facilitating the dissection. The anterior colpotomy is nearly complete, with only a small amount of vaginal epithelium remaining. The posterior colpotomy is performed, and the uterus is successfully pulled out along with the colpotomizer.

The surgeon performs suction irrigation to clean the surgical area, ensuring there is no bleeding. The right side salpingectomy is conducted due to the presence of a small ovarian cyst. The fallopian tube is manipulated, with the right side being addressed first, followed by the left side.

The surgeon lifts the anterior fornix and places the tube and ovary into the vagina using stone holding forceps. A needle is dropped through the vagina to start closing the posterior vaginal vault, including the utero sacral ligament, with full thickness sutures. The anterior loop is also included in the intracorporeal continuous suture technique.

The assistant lifts the bladder peritoneum to expose the vaginal plane. The surgeon includes the left corner and uterosacral ligament in the dissection, approximating the vaginal fascia and epithelium edges together with a suture knot, which is then cut.
